subject: Arrows
method: full human/LLM read of transcript 030 (not the heuristic)
note: All 21 tiers below were confirmed to match the stored tierlists.json exactly. The 'why' field is new content extracted from the transcript - the app currently stores no reasoning. Barbed Arrow and Arrow of Piercing are deliberately excluded by the author (unique, tiny quantities).

Arrow of Acid.why: Acid surface gives a no-save -2 AC to whatever you hit, effectively free archery fighting style; damage is irrelevant but the to-hit boost is huge for Sharpshooter archers.

Arrow of Fire.why: Fire surface deals a guaranteed 1d4 even on a miss and can stack in packed fights; a solid default but falls off as enemies gain fire resistance.

Arrow of Ice.why: Ice surface forces a prone save that can burn whole enemy turns and break concentration, plus strong surface-combo synergies (steam, water, electrified).

Arrow of Lightning.why: Identical to the other elemental arrows but creates no surface, so it's only a small chance at marginal damage - not worth stockpiling.

Arrow of Beast Slaying.why: Doubles damage vs beasts, but only sold late in Act 1 after the hardest beast fights are over.

Arrow of Construct Slaying.why: Great for the dangerous Act 3 construct fights, though piercing resistance and single-instance-damage mechanics blunt it somewhat.

Arrow of Elemental Slaying.why: Sold from Act 2; misses the deadly early elementals but shines in a couple of dangerous late-game elemental encounters.

Arrow of Fiend Slaying.why: Fiends are common and mostly filler, but the arrow is very useful in the few genuinely hard fiend fights.

Arrow of Monstrosity Slaying.why: Monstrosities cluster in one Act 1 zone and are rarely threatening; usefulness depends entirely on play order.

Arrow of Undead Slaying.why: Undead appear in huge numbers across all three acts including some of the hardest fights - grab as many as possible.

Arrow of Aberration Slaying.why: Surprisingly few aberration fights, but they're high-priority targets in the most important encounters, so doubling damage matters.

Arrow of Dragon Slaying.why: Only a handful of dragon fights, but they're among the deadliest, and you can stockpile enough to use exclusively against them.

Arrow of Humanoid Slaying.why: About half the enemies in the game are humanoid, so this comes up constantly - stockpile for every party.

Arrow of Darkness.why: Casts a 3-turn no-concentration Darkness as an attack action, breaking action economy; S+ for darkness/warlock builds, merely S otherwise.

Arrow of Ilmater.why: Stops healing for a turn (good vs natural regenerators) plus a weak hit-only 1d4 necrotic; situational, occasionally backfires by denying the AI a wasted heal.

Arrow of Roaring Thunder.why: No bonus damage, low strength-save knockback that enemies resist easily, and often forces you to forgo hitting an enemy to aim it.

Arrow of Arcane Interference.why: Guaranteed no-save concentration break is decent (great for non-archers), but the intended silence barely functions due to BG3's duration timing.

Arrow of Many Targets.why: Hits up to 4 targets with near-full damage each, multiplies crit chances and on-hit stacks (fastest Arcane Acuity stacking) - roughly 3.5x damage, wipes encounters.

Arrow of Salving.why: Always-hit ally heal that removes poison/paralysis/blind as an attack - a rare but valuable panic button, especially for characters without lesser restoration.

Arrow of Smokepowder.why: A free Fireball-on-an-arrow (8d6 split fire/force) plus knockback, no spell slot - a third-level spell as an attack action, massive damage.

Arrow of Transposition.why: Free 60ft Misty Step as an attack; rarely your primary mobility (a bonus action is usually better) but great to keep one on every character.
